title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 10
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness. Continue working in the same comp331-java-agent repository you used for Labs 1-4; do not create a new repository for this lab.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Implement the core agent loop
Design controlled multi-step workflows
Introduce human oversight into your coding agent
Move from simple workflows to agent-like behavior
Combine the Java CLI, message history, sandbox tools, evaluation, and safety controls from Labs 1-4

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository and review the tool layer and risk mitigations from Labs 3-4. Continue in that repository instead of creating a new one.
Implement the controlled agent loop, tool registry connection, approval checkpoint, and example workflow described in the linked instructions.
Preserve the required workflow artifacts, including prompt, tool calls, approvals, changed files, evaluation result, and final response.
Document reproducible setup, Copilot-assisted workflow decisions, and verification results in the repository.
Commit your completed work and obtain the Git commit hash for the submitted version.
Submit the repository link, commit hash, and reproduction notes through Canvas.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot to help design and debug the controlled workflow, but preserve human oversight. Copilot may suggest unsafe automation; your implementation must keep approval checkpoints, command restrictions, and iteration limits.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot to help draft the loop stages before implementation.
Use Copilot to generate pseudocode, then manually adapt it to your existing architecture.
Ask Copilot to review whether your human approval checkpoints are meaningful.
Use Copilot to debug one controlled workflow run, but verify the final behavior with tests or a reproducible transcript.
Preserve a transcript or notes showing Copilot-assisted design, debugging, or review of the agent loop.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Runnable Java controlled coding agent workflow.
Documentation of the agent loop stages.
Tool schema or tool description documentation.
Tool dispatcher or registry connected to the Lab 3 tools.
Human oversight checkpoint description and evidence that it is used.
Example run artifacts, including prompt, tool calls, tool outputs, approvals, evaluation result, and final decision.
Reproducible setup and execution instructions.
A short limitations section that connects back to the Lab 4 risk analysis.
Copilot transcript or notes showing how Copilot helped design, debug, or review the controlled workflow.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
6 Grading
Refer to the assignment rubric in Canvas.

title: Lab 5: Coding Agents and Controlled Workflows
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link through Canvas. Clearly identify the command, script, or steps your professor should use to reproduce the controlled agent workflow. Submit the Git commit hash for the exact version you want graded. After committing your final work, run:
git rev-parse HEAD

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 7
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development. Continue working in the same comp331-java-agent repository you used for Labs 1-2; do not create a new repository for this lab.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Integrate components into a working coding agent workflow
Connect structured input, AI generation, and evaluation
Transition from isolated tasks to a cohesive system
Implement safe Java tools that allow an agent to inspect and work inside a sandbox

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Integrate your structured input and evaluation work in your existing comp331-java-agent repository by adding Java tool implementations. These tools are the controlled operations your coding agent will later expose to the LLM. At this stage, you may test the tools directly from Java code or JUnit; the model does not need to call them automatically yet.

Define a common tool interface or base pattern. For example, you might create an AgentTool interface with methods such as name(), description(), and execute(...).
Define one sandbox working directory. This should point to the Java sandbox project from Lab 2, such as workspace/calculator.
Implement a file listing tool similar to get_files_info. It should list files and directories plus basic metadata such as size and whether each entry is a directory.
Implement a file reading tool similar to get_file_content. It should read text files from the sandbox and cap output at a reasonable length, such as 10,000 characters.
Implement a file writing tool similar to write_file. It should create or overwrite files only inside the sandbox.
Implement a command execution tool similar to run_python_file, adapted for Java. Recommended: RunJavaTestsTool that runs mvn test or gradle test in the sandbox. Avoid arbitrary shell access unless you have a strict allowlist.
Use Java Path APIs to prevent path traversal. Paths such as ../README.md or absolute paths outside the sandbox must be rejected.
Add JUnit tests for each tool. Include successful cases and blocked cases for missing files, invalid directories, path traversal, and command restrictions.
Add a small manual driver or test command that demonstrates the tools working together: list files, read a Java file, write a harmless file, and run the sandbox tests.
Document the architecture and current limitations in your README.

Suggested Java path-safety approach:
Path sandbox = Path.of("workspace/calculator").toAbsolutePath().normalize();
Path target = sandbox.resolve(userProvidedPath).normalize();
if (!target.startsWith(sandbox)) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException("Path is outside the sandbox");
}
Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="--tool-demo"

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 3: AI System Implementation.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository and review the Lab 2 sandbox directory. Continue in that repository instead of creating a new one.
Implement the Java tool layer, sandbox restrictions, tool tests, and demo described in the linked instructions.
Document the tool architecture, sample run, accepted/rejected Copilot suggestions, and verification results in README.md.
Commit your completed work and obtain the Git commit hash for the submitted version.
Submit the repository link, commit hash, and required tool demo or test evidence through Canvas.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot as a design and review assistant for the tool layer. Be especially cautious with file and command tools; Copilot may suggest implementations that are too permissive.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot Chat to brainstorm a small Java interface for tools before coding.
Use Copilot to draft tests for path traversal, missing files, blocked absolute paths, and command allowlists.
Ask Copilot to review your implementation for sandbox escape risks.
Document at least one Copilot suggestion you rejected because it was unsafe, overbroad, or insufficiently tested.
Verify accepted suggestions with JUnit tests and the suggested checkpoint commands.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Working Java source code for the agent tool layer.
Tools for listing files, reading files, writing files, and running Java evaluation commands.
Sandbox path validation that blocks access outside the permitted working directory.
JUnit tests for successful and blocked tool behavior.
Architecture documentation in the README or a separate design document.
A sample tool demo or transcript showing at least one successful workflow.
Evaluation results from at least one sandbox test run.
Copilot notes describing one accepted suggestion, one rejected or revised suggestion, and the verification used.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
6 Grading
Refer to the assignment rubric in Canvas.

title: Lab 3: AI System Implementation
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link through Canvas. Include any notes your professor needs to run the tool tests or demo workflow. Submit the Git commit hash for the exact version you want graded. After committing your final work, run:
git rev-parse HEAD
Next lab: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 3
Points: 90
Complete this lab using a structured AI-assisted workflow.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Establish the task input layer of your coding agent
Translate feature requests into structured specifications
Compare naive vs structured AI-assisted development
Begin building the foundation of a Java-based coding agent system

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Build the first layer of a Controlled Coding Agent workflow: a Java command-line program that can accept a user request, send it to an LLM, and compare naive prompting with structured prompting. This lab should feel small and controlled. You are not building the full agent yet.
Start fresh for this lab. Create a new repository named comp331-java-agent. Do not use your Assignment 1 setup repository as the starting point unless your professor explicitly approves it. Create this lab repository once; it is the continuing repository for Labs 1-5 and may become the starting point for your final project.

Create a fresh GitHub repository named comp331-java-agent. You will continue developing in this same repository for Labs 2-5; do not create a new comp331-java-agent repository for each lab.
Create a Java project using Maven in that repository. A simple structure such as src/main/java/.../Main.java is enough.
Configure your Gemini API key using the Gemini API Setup Guide. Do not commit API keys, .env files, or other secrets.
Write a Main class that first sends one hard-coded prompt to the model and prints the response. This verifies that your Java project can connect to the API.
Revise the program so it accepts the user prompt from command-line arguments, for example mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="Explain this Java method".
Add a simple message representation for later labs. This may be a small Message class or record with fields such as role and content.
Add a --verbose flag or similar option that prints helpful debugging information, such as the user prompt, selected model, and token counts if your API provides them.
Choose a small Java feature request that can be implemented in a utility class, such as formatting a string, validating input, parsing a simple expression, or computing a value.
Ask GitHub Copilot or another approved coding assistant for help once using a naive prompt or informal comment. Save the prompt and resulting code or describe the interaction.
Create a structured task specification with goal, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria. Use that specification to revise or re-implement the feature.
Compare the naive and structured approaches in your README. Focus on concrete differences in output quality, missing edge cases, and human decisions.

Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="Why are structured prompts useful for Java development?"
mvn exec:java -Dexec.args="--verbose Explain what this program does"

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Go to github.com and create a fresh repository named comp331-java-agent. This is the one lab repository you will continue using for Labs 1-5.
Build the Java LLM command-line client and naive vs structured Copilot comparison described in the linked instructions.
Document setup, run commands, prompts, accepted/rejected Copilot suggestions, comparison notes, and verification results in README.md.
Commit your completed work and obtain the Git commit hash for the submitted version.
Submit the repository link and commit hash through Canvas.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot to compare unstructured assistance with intentional, structured assistance. You remain responsible for understanding, testing, and explaining all submitted work.
Required Copilot workflow:

Use inline Copilot once with a vague comment or naive prompt. Save the prompt/comment and result.
Use Copilot Chat again with a structured task specification. Include goal, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, and acceptance criteria.
Ask Copilot to explain the structured result before accepting it.
Compare the naive and structured outputs in your README. Focus on correctness, missing edge cases, and what you changed manually.

Suggested structured prompt:
You are helping me implement a small Java utility method.
Goal:
Inputs:
Outputs:
Constraints:
Edge cases:
Acceptance criteria:
Generate only the method and a short explanation.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

The Lab 1 repository named comp331-java-agent, which will continue as your working repository for Labs 2-5.
A runnable Java Maven project with a command-line entry point.
Code that sends a prompt to an LLM from Java.
Command-line prompt handling and a verbose/debug mode.
Java source code for both the naive and structured feature attempts, or clearly separated commits showing each approach.
A structured task specification.
A README.md explaining setup, API key configuration, run commands, and the naive vs structured comparison.
Copilot evidence showing the naive interaction, structured interaction, accepted changes, rejected or revised suggestions, and verification method.
Evidence of AI-assisted development through prompts, comments, screenshots, transcript snippets, or log links.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
6 Grading
Refer to the assignment rubric in Canvas.

title: Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Share the comp331-java-agent Git repository with your professor per their instructions and submit the repository link through Canvas. Include any setup notes needed to run your Java project. Submit the Git commit hash for the exact version you want graded. After committing your final work, run:
git rev-parse HEAD
Next lab: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Learning Outcomes

Evaluate emerging AI-assisted development trends.
Synthesize course concepts into a framework.
Propose improvements to AI-assisted workflows.
Formulate a personal engineering approach to AI use.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Beyond Code Generation
AI assistance is expanding into:

Test generation.
Debugging.
Maintenance.
Design exploration.
Project coordination.
Agent-driven task execution.

The engineering problem remains: how do we trust the result?

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Course Concept Map

flowchart TD
  I[Intent] --> P[Prompting]
  P --> E[Evaluation]
  E --> T[Testing]
  T --> R[Risk analysis]
  R --> A[Agent workflows]
  A --> C[Capstone system]
  H[Human judgment] --> I
  H --> E
  H --> R
  H --> C




flowchart TD
  I[Intent] --> P[Prompting]
  P --> E[Evaluation]
  E --> T[Testing]
  T --> R[Risk analysis]
  R --> A[Agent workflows]
  A --> C[Capstone system]
  H[Human judgment] --> I
  H --> E
  H --> R
  H --> C

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Future Skill Shift
Important skills move toward:

Problem framing.
Requirements clarification.
Architecture judgment.
Test design.
Review and verification.
Workflow design.
Ethical decision-making.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Essence of Good Design
Thomas and Hunt define good design around ease of change.
AI-era interpretation:

Good systems absorb generated changes safely.
Good boundaries make AI suggestions reviewable.
Good tests make iteration cheap.
Good documentation makes context reusable.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Personal AI Workflow
A durable workflow should answer:

When do I ask AI for help?
How do I scope the request?
What evidence must I collect?
What do I never delegate?
How do I improve after each interaction?

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Course Framework
The course has built a progression:

Intent.
Prompting.
Evaluation.
Human judgment.
Testing.
Production readiness.
Systems thinking.
Security.
Ethics.
Agents.
Capstone integration.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Milestone 2 Connection
Your partial implementation should prove the final system is feasible.
Evidence should include:

Meaningful implementation.
Component integration.
Example workflow run.
Evaluation strategy.
Risks and limitations.
Peer review and AI notes.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Improving the Workflow
Ask your team:

Which prompts produced useful output?
Which AI suggestions created rework?
Which tests found real issues?
Which review practices improved quality?
Which risks are still unmanaged?

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Studio Activity
Create a personal AI-assisted SWE checklist with three sections:

Before prompting.
Before accepting output.
Before submitting or merging.

Tie each item to course evidence.

title: Module 11: The Future of AI Engineering
text:
Takeaways

The future is not just faster coding.
The future is better orchestration, verification, and judgment.
Your professional advantage is disciplined AI use.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Learning Outcomes

Evaluate ethical risks in AI-assisted development.
Assess responsibility boundaries between engineers and AI systems.
Analyze bias and unintended consequences.
Justify responsible engineering practices.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Responsibility Boundary
AI can assist, but it cannot be accountable.
The engineer or team remains responsible for:

Submitted code.
Security impacts.
Documentation accuracy.
Disclosure of AI assistance.
User consequences.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Accountability Flow

flowchart LR
  A[AI suggestion] --> B[Student review]
  B --> C[Team PR review]
  C --> D[Tests and evidence]
  D --> E[Submission]
  E --> F[Accountability remains with humans]




flowchart LR
  A[AI suggestion] --> B[Student review]
  B --> C[Team PR review]
  C --> D[Tests and evidence]
  D --> E[Submission]
  E --> F[Accountability remains with humans]

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Transparency
Responsible AI use includes a clear artifact trail.

What tool or mode was used?
What prompt or task was given?
What was accepted, modified, or rejected?
How was the result verified?
What uncertainty remains?

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
IP and Provenance
AI-generated output can raise provenance questions.
Practical safeguards:

Avoid asking for copyrighted code.
Be cautious with suspiciously specific output.
Prefer explanations, patterns, and tests over copied solutions.
Follow course and organizational policy.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Bias and Consequences
Software can encode unfair assumptions.
AI can amplify them by producing common patterns without context.
Ask:

Who could be harmed?
Whose data is missing?
What assumptions are hidden?
What feedback would reveal harm?

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Pragmatic Teams
Thomas and Hunt emphasize stable, communicative teams with shared standards.
For AI-assisted teams:

Agree on AI usage rules.
Review AI-generated code carefully.
Share prompt patterns that work.
Do not let AI create invisible solo work.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Controlled Collaboration
Team repositories should show:

Issues or task board.
Feature branches.
Pull requests.
Review comments.
Test evidence.
AI usage notes.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Lab 5 Development Connection
Coding agents increase ethical stakes because they act over multiple steps.
Controlled workflows need:

Human checkpoints.
Reproducible runs.
Tool boundaries.
Reviewable artifacts.
Clear stop conditions.

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Studio Activity
Draft team AI rules:

When is Copilot allowed?
What must be documented?
What requires peer review?
What data must never be pasted?
What counts as sufficient verification?

title: Module 9: Ethics and Responsibility
text:
Takeaways

Responsible use is an engineering practice, not a slogan.
Transparency supports grading, collaboration, and trust.
Teams need explicit norms for AI-assisted work.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Learning Outcomes

Analyze components of an AI-assisted system.
Describe data and context flow.
Evaluate integration challenges.
Design a basic architecture for an AI-assisted feature.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
From Task to System
An AI-assisted system is more than a model call.
It includes:

Input handling.
Context assembly.
Prompt construction.
Model interaction.
Tool invocation.
Evaluation.
Artifact tracking.
Human oversight.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Controlled Agent Architecture

flowchart TD
  U[User request] --> S[Structured task spec]
  S --> C[Context builder]
  C --> M[Model interaction]
  M --> T[Tool dispatcher]
  T --> X[Sandboxed tools]
  X --> R[Tool results]
  R --> E[Evaluation]
  E --> A[Artifacts and logs]
  E --> H[Human checkpoint]
  H --> M




flowchart TD
  U[User request] --> S[Structured task spec]
  S --> C[Context builder]
  C --> M[Model interaction]
  M --> T[Tool dispatcher]
  T --> X[Sandboxed tools]
  X --> R[Tool results]
  R --> E[Evaluation]
  E --> A[Artifacts and logs]
  E --> H[Human checkpoint]
  H --> M

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
System Boundary
Define what the agent may and may not do.
For a controlled coding agent:

Allowed tools.
Allowed paths.
Allowed commands.
Required approvals.
Required logs.
Required tests.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Context Flow
Context should be curated, not dumped.
Typical flow:

User request.
Structured task specification.
Relevant project files or summaries.
Tool definitions.
Evaluation criteria.
Output and artifact records.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Integration Challenges

Mismatched frontend/backend contracts.
Unclear data models.
Generated code that bypasses abstractions.
Tests that require unavailable services.
Tool outputs too verbose to inspect.
Error handling split across layers.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Tracer Bullet Architecture
Build one thin complete workflow first.
Example:

Read task.
Ask model for plan.
Dispatch one safe tool.
Capture result.
Evaluate with one check.
Save run artifact.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
AI System Prompting
Useful prompts ask the model to operate inside architecture.

“Use only these tools.”
“Return JSON matching this schema.”
“If input is ambiguous, ask for clarification.”
“Do not modify files outside this sandbox.”
“Explain verification steps separately from implementation.”

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Lab 4 Development Connection
Lab 4 begins risk analysis and production readiness.
Your risk analysis should be specific to your actual system:

Tool misuse.
Sandbox escape.
Prompt injection.
False confidence from weak tests.
Missing human checkpoint.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Studio Activity
Draw your agent as boxes and arrows.
For each arrow, label:

Data passed.
Trust boundary.
Validation step.
Failure mode.

title: Module 7: Building AI Systems
text:
Takeaways

AI engineering is systems engineering.
Context, tools, and evaluation are first-class components.
Thin end-to-end slices make risk visible.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Learning Outcomes

Analyze AI-generated code for correctness and quality.
Identify common failure patterns.
Distinguish superficial correctness from true correctness.
Apply testing strategies to validate outputs.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Review, Refine, Own
Osmani’s core message for generated code: understanding is not optional.
You own:

What was generated.
What was accepted.
What was changed.
What was verified.
What risk remains.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Review Pipeline

flowchart LR
  A[AI-generated code] --> B[Read the diff]
  B --> C[Check requirements]
  C --> D[Run tests]
  D --> E[Add missing tests]
  E --> F[Refactor]
  F --> G[Document decision]
  D -->|failure| H[Revise prompt or code]
  H --> B




flowchart LR
  A[AI-generated code] --> B[Read the diff]
  B --> C[Check requirements]
  C --> D[Run tests]
  D --> E[Add missing tests]
  E --> F[Refactor]
  F --> G[Document decision]
  D -->|failure| H[Revise prompt or code]
  H --> B

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Superficial Correctness
Code may appear correct because it:

Matches naming conventions.
Compiles.
Handles the example input.
Uses familiar libraries.
Includes comments that sound confident.

None of these prove behavior.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Review Checklist

Does it implement the stated requirement?
What assumptions did it add?
What edge cases are missing?
Is error handling intentional?
Is the design testable?
Does it expose secrets or unsafe behavior?
Are dependencies appropriate?

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Tests as Design
Thomas and Hunt argue testing is part of design, not an afterthought.
For AI-assisted work:

Ask for tests before accepting implementation.
Review tests as critically as code.
Avoid tests that merely mirror the implementation.
Add regression tests for AI mistakes.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Test Pyramid for AI Code

Unit tests: fast checks of isolated behavior.
Integration tests: verify components work together.
End-to-end tests: validate user-visible workflow.
Safety tests: verify refusal, bounds, permissions, and secrets handling.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Property Thinking
Property-based testing asks what must always be true.
Examples for Java command parsing:

Parsing then serializing preserves arguments.
Invalid commands never execute tools.
Sandbox paths never escape the workspace.
Empty input always fails safely.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Lab 3 Development Connection
As Lab 3 begins, implementation must remain inspectable.
Good evidence includes:

Tool contracts.
Sandbox restrictions.
Tests proving restrictions.
Sample runs.
Copilot decisions and corrections.

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Studio Activity
Give Copilot an intentionally incomplete implementation and ask it to review.
Then do a human review and compare:

What did Copilot catch?
What did it miss?
Which issues require domain judgment?

title: Module 5: Reviewing and Testing AI Code
text:
Takeaways

AI-generated code needs adversarial review.
Tests are engineering evidence.
Owning code means understanding failure behavior.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Learning Outcomes

Describe the 70 percent problem.
Apply structured workflows to guide generation.
Compare naive prompting with structured approaches.
Implement a basic feature using an AI-assisted workflow.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
What Is the 70 Percent Problem?
AI output often looks close enough to create confidence but not close enough to trust.
Common shape:

Compiles, but misses edge cases.
Solves the sample, but not the requirement.
Uses plausible APIs incorrectly.
Produces code that is hard to maintain.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
The 70 Percent Trap

flowchart LR
  A[Looks plausible] --> B[Accepted too early]
  B --> C[Hidden defects]
  C --> D[Debugging debt]
  D --> E[Lost trust]
  A --> F[Structured review]
  F --> G[Tests expose gaps]
  G --> H[Safer iteration]




flowchart LR
  A[Looks plausible] --> B[Accepted too early]
  B --> C[Hidden defects]
  C --> D[Debugging debt]
  D --> E[Lost trust]
  A --> F[Structured review]
  F --> G[Tests expose gaps]
  G --> H[Safer iteration]

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Why It Is Dangerous
Partially correct output can be worse than obviously wrong output.

It saves time early.
It shifts cost into debugging and review.
It creates sunk-cost pressure.
It can hide security or reliability defects.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Workflow Over Heroics
Effective AI-assisted development uses a loop:

Define intent.
Ask for a small implementation.
Inspect the diff.
Run tests.
Refine prompt or code.
Document decisions.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Tracer Bullets
Thomas and Hunt recommend building thin, end-to-end slices that prove direction.
In AI-assisted SWE:

Ask AI to help create the smallest complete path.
Keep scope narrow enough to evaluate.
Prefer inspectable progress over broad unfinished generation.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Bad AI Workflow

Ask for a whole feature.
Paste output without reading.
Fix compile errors manually.
Skip tests because it “seems right.”
Document only the final result.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Better AI Workflow

Ask for an implementation plan first.
Generate one component at a time.
Require tests before or alongside code.
Compare output against criteria.
Preserve rejected suggestions and rationale.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Lab 2 Development Connection
Eval-driven development starts this week.
Your lab goal is to make correctness measurable before relying on AI output.
Evaluation criteria should be:

Specific.
Runnable or inspectable.
Connected to task intent.
Useful for regression.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Studio Activity
Given a Java feature request, define:

One happy-path test.
Two edge-case tests.
One invalid-input test.
One maintainability criterion.

Then ask Copilot to implement only enough code to pass those checks.

title: Module 3: The 70 Percent Problem
text:
Takeaways

The last 30 percent is engineering work.
Small slices expose uncertainty early.
AI is most useful when surrounded by feedback loops.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Learning Outcomes

Explain the difference between vibe coding and AI-assisted software engineering.
Describe programming with intent.
Summarize strengths and limits of AI-generated code.
Interpret Software 2.0 as a shift in specification and verification.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
The Core Shift
AI changes the interface to software creation.
It does not remove the need for engineering judgment.

Before: translate intent into code directly.
Now: translate intent into prompts, examples, constraints, tests, reviews, and code.
The human role moves upstream and downstream: specify better, verify harder.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
AI-Assisted SWE Loop

flowchart LR
  A[Human intent] --> B[Structured prompt]
  B --> C[AI draft]
  C --> D[Human review]
  D --> E[Tests and evidence]
  E --> F[Commit and document]
  E -->|gaps found| B




flowchart LR
  A[Human intent] --> B[Structured prompt]
  B --> C[AI draft]
  C --> D[Human review]
  D --> E[Tests and evidence]
  E --> F[Commit and document]
  E -->|gaps found| B

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Vibe Coding vs Engineering



Vibe Coding
AI-Assisted Engineering




Conversational exploration
Structured collaboration


Accepts plausible output
Verifies behavior and risks


Optimizes for momentum
Optimizes for trusted progress


Weak artifact trail
Prompts, tests, decisions, commits

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Programming With Intent
Programming with intent means making the desired behavior explicit before accepting implementation.

What should the system do?
What should it refuse to do?
What edge cases matter?
What evidence proves it works?
What human decision remains non-delegable?

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Pragmatic Foundation
Thomas and Hunt frame software mastery as responsibility, communication, and continuous adaptation.
For AI-assisted SWE, this means:

Own every generated artifact.
Communicate precisely with humans and tools.
Treat AI output as a draft, not authority.
Keep improving your knowledge portfolio.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Where AI Helps

Drafting boilerplate and examples.
Exploring alternate implementations.
Explaining unfamiliar APIs.
Creating first-pass tests and docs.
Speeding up setup and debugging paths.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Where AI Still Struggles

Hidden requirements and domain context.
Architecture tradeoffs across time.
Security-sensitive boundary decisions.
Correctness when tests are weak.
Knowing when the task is underspecified.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Software 2.0 Lens
Software 2.0 reframes some development as training, prompting, selecting, and evaluating behavior.
In this course, we apply that lens pragmatically:

Specifications become prompts plus tests.
Code review includes model-output review.
Engineering evidence matters more than confidence.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Setup Lab Connection
Assignment 1 is not just tool setup.
It establishes the course habit:

Capture the prompt.
Inspect the suggestion.
Make a manual decision.
Verify the result.
Document what happened.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Studio Activity
Ask Copilot for a small Java method.
Then improve the interaction:

Add input constraints.
Add expected behavior.
Add one edge case.
Ask for a JUnit test.
Record what changed between attempts.

title: Module 1: What AI Changes
text:
Takeaways

AI assistance amplifies both good and bad engineering habits.
Intent, verification, and responsibility remain human work.
The course evidence trail is part of the engineering process.

title: Lecture Downloads
text: Download the PDF for the module you need, then open it in any PDF reader. The HTML version is best for presenting in a browser. The source files can be edited with Quarto if an instructor wants to customize the slides.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Learning Outcomes

Explain prompting as specification.
Apply prompt structures that improve output quality.
Construct prompts with constraints and expected behavior.
Iterate based on model responses.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Prompting Is Engineering Communication
A prompt is not a magic phrase.
It is a communication artifact that should carry:

Goal.
Context.
Constraints.
Examples.
Output format.
Verification expectations.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Prompt Anatomy

flowchart TD
  P[Effective prompt] --> G[Goal]
  P --> C[Context]
  P --> R[Requirements]
  P --> X[Constraints]
  P --> E[Examples]
  P --> V[Verification]




flowchart TD
  P[Effective prompt] --> G[Goal]
  P --> C[Context]
  P --> R[Requirements]
  P --> X[Constraints]
  P --> E[Examples]
  P --> V[Verification]

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Weak Prompt Pattern
“Write a Java parser for commands.”
Problems:

No grammar.
No failure behavior.
No test cases.
No integration context.
No definition of done.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Strong Prompt Pattern
“Create a Java method that parses commands in the form toolName arg1 arg2. Requirements: reject blank tool names, preserve quoted arguments, return a typed result, throw IllegalArgumentException for malformed input, and include JUnit 5 tests for valid input, blank input, unmatched quotes, and extra whitespace.”

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Prompt Components

Role: what expertise should the assistant apply?
Task: what should be produced?
Context: what codebase or assignment constraints matter?
Constraints: what must not happen?
Examples: what should inputs and outputs look like?
Evaluation: how will we know it is correct?

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Iterative Refinement
Osmani emphasizes prompt iteration as a feedback loop.
In software terms:

Prompt is the initial spec.
Output is the prototype.
Review identifies gaps.
Follow-up prompt narrows ambiguity.
Tests convert expectations into evidence.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Pragmatic Paranoia
Pragmatic paranoia means assuming things can fail and designing accordingly.
For AI-assisted work:

Ask for failure cases.
Ask what assumptions the answer made.
Ask for tests that should fail against a naive solution.
Do not accept code without a verification path.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Tool Fluency
The Basic Tools matter more with AI, not less.

Git lets you isolate and inspect changes.
Shell commands make verification repeatable.
IDEs provide context and refactoring support.
Logs and diffs reveal what the assistant actually changed.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Lab 1 Connection
Lab 1 compares naive and structured workflows.
Your evidence should show:

The naive prompt and output.
The structured specification.
Accepted, rejected, and revised suggestions.
Verification results.
Why the structured approach changed the outcome.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Studio Activity
Take a vague feature request and rewrite it three ways:

User-story prompt.
Test-first prompt.
Risk-aware prompt.

Compare what each prompt asks the AI to optimize for.

title: Module 2: Prompting as Communication
text:
Takeaways

Prompt quality is specification quality.
Constraints reduce ambiguity.
Iteration is expected, not failure.
Verification turns conversation into engineering.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Learning Outcomes

Identify where human judgment is critical.
Analyze the role of system design and architecture.
Explain how domain knowledge impacts output quality.
Evaluate AI limitations in complex engineering tasks.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
The Human 30 Percent
Osmani frames the engineer’s durable value as the work AI cannot reliably infer.

What matters in this domain?
What tradeoff is acceptable?
What future change should the design tolerate?
What risk is not visible in the code?

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Where Human Judgment Concentrates

mindmap
  root((Human judgment))
    Domain context
    Architecture
    Tradeoffs
    Risk tolerance
    Long-term maintenance
    User consequences




mindmap
  root((Human judgment))
    Domain context
    Architecture
    Tradeoffs
    Risk tolerance
    Long-term maintenance
    User consequences

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Architect and Editor
AI can draft local code quickly.
Humans still own:

Boundaries.
Naming.
Abstractions.
Data flow.
Operational constraints.
Long-term maintainability.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Bend or Break
Pragmatic design favors systems that can change.
For AI-generated code, watch for:

Tight coupling.
Hidden global state.
Over-specific implementations.
Missing seams for tests.
Configuration hardcoded into logic.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Context Is a Design Input
AI performs better when given project context, but context is never complete.
Human engineers supply:

Domain vocabulary.
Nonfunctional requirements.
Organizational constraints.
Legacy system knowledge.
User consequences.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Concurrency as a Cautionary Case
Concurrent systems reveal AI limits because correctness depends on interactions over time.
Ask:

What state is shared?
What order assumptions exist?
What happens under failure?
Can tests expose race conditions?

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Architecture Prompt Pattern
Ask AI for alternatives, not the answer.
Useful structure:

“Generate three designs.”
“List tradeoffs for reliability, testability, and complexity.”
“Identify assumptions.”
“Recommend one for this course scope.”
“Show what would make that recommendation wrong.”

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Lab 2 Final Connection
Your eval-driven lab should demonstrate human judgment through:

Measurable criteria.
Runnable checks.
Regression comparison.
Documentation explaining what the tests prove and do not prove.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Studio Activity
Review AI-generated Java code and mark:

Local correctness issues.
Design issues.
Missing tests.
Hidden assumptions.
Questions you would ask before merging.

title: Module 4: Human Contribution Beyond AI
text:
Takeaways

AI can accelerate implementation but cannot own architecture.
Human value concentrates in judgment, context, and verification.
Flexible design protects you from fast wrong turns.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Learning Outcomes

Distinguish prototype and production systems.
Analyze tradeoffs between speed and reliability.
Identify gaps between generated code and production needs.
Explain strategies for evolving prototypes into production systems.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Prototype Value
AI is strong at rapid prototyping because it can quickly create visible progress.
Prototype goals:

Learn feasibility.
Expose requirement gaps.
Compare alternatives.
Get feedback early.

Prototype risk: mistaking a learning artifact for a production system.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Prototype to Production Path

flowchart LR
  A[Prototype] --> B[Feedback]
  B --> C[Tests]
  C --> D[Refactor boundaries]
  D --> E[Security review]
  E --> F[Observability]
  F --> G[Production candidate]
  C -->|fails| A




flowchart LR
  A[Prototype] --> B[Feedback]
  B --> C[Tests]
  C --> D[Refactor boundaries]
  D --> E[Security review]
  E --> F[Observability]
  F --> G[Production candidate]
  C -->|fails| A

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Production Means Constraints
Production-ready software must address:

Reliability.
Security.
Observability.
Maintainability.
Dependency management.
Error recovery.
Documentation.
Operational cost.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Prototype to Production Gap
AI-generated prototypes often omit:

Input validation.
Permission boundaries.
Logging discipline.
Test coverage.
Failure modes.
Deployment assumptions.
Data migration concerns.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Orthogonality
Orthogonal components can change independently.
For the Java agent project:

Prompt construction should not depend on CLI parsing.
Tool dispatch should not depend on UI output.
Evaluation should run without live model calls.
Secret handling should be isolated.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Reversibility
Decisions should be easy to change when uncertainty is high.
Use AI to explore reversible options:

Interfaces before implementations.
Adapter boundaries around model providers.
Configurable paths and tools.
Small commits that can be discarded.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Production Readiness Questions

What can fail?
How will we know?
What is the safe default?
What should be logged?
What must never be logged?
What requires human approval?

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Lab 3 Final Connection
Lab 3 implementation should prove that your system is controlled, not just functional.
Evidence should include:

Required Java tools.
Sandbox enforcement.
Inspectable output.
Architecture notes.
Evaluation results.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Studio Activity
Take a working prototype feature and label each part:

Keep for production.
Rewrite before production.
Add tests.
Add monitoring.
Add security review.

title: Module 6: Prototyping vs Production
text:
Takeaways

AI speed is useful for learning, not a substitute for readiness.
Orthogonal design makes generated code safer to evolve.
Reversibility reduces the cost of AI-assisted experiments.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Learning Outcomes

Identify security risks in AI-generated code.
Analyze reliability challenges.
Evaluate safety, performance, and complexity tradeoffs.
Propose strategies to improve robustness.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
AI-Specific Risk Pattern
AI often generates code optimized for the visible task, not the threat model.
Risks include:

Missing authorization checks.
Insecure defaults.
Unsafe command execution.
Overbroad file access.
Dependency confusion.
Logging secrets.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Risk Matrix

quadrantChart
  title Risk Prioritization
  x-axis Low likelihood --> High likelihood
  y-axis Low impact --> High impact
  quadrant-1 Mitigate first
  quadrant-2 Monitor
  quadrant-3 Accept or document
  quadrant-4 Prevent cheaply
  Command injection: [0.80, 0.90]
  Path traversal: [0.65, 0.80]
  Weak tests: [0.75, 0.60]
  Verbose logs: [0.45, 0.55]
  Formatting issue: [0.30, 0.20]




quadrantChart
  title Risk Prioritization
  x-axis Low likelihood --> High likelihood
  y-axis Low impact --> High impact
  quadrant-1 Mitigate first
  quadrant-2 Monitor
  quadrant-3 Accept or document
  quadrant-4 Prevent cheaply
  Command injection: [0.80, 0.90]
  Path traversal: [0.65, 0.80]
  Weak tests: [0.75, 0.60]
  Verbose logs: [0.45, 0.55]
  Formatting issue: [0.30, 0.20]

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Security Review Areas

Input validation.
Authentication and authorization.
Secrets handling.
Dependency versions.
Path traversal.
Command injection.
Network access.
Audit logs.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Reliability Review Areas

Timeouts.
Retries and backoff.
Deterministic test paths.
Clear failure states.
Idempotent operations.
Recoverable artifacts.
Observability.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Refactoring AI Code
Refactoring turns generated code into maintainable code without changing behavior.
Use tests first, then improve:

Names.
Duplication.
Boundaries.
Error handling.
Configuration.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Estimating Risk
Risk ratings should be concrete.
Use a simple scale:

Likelihood: low, medium, high.
Impact: low, medium, high.
Mitigation: specific action.
Evidence: test, review, log, or policy.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Safeguard Examples

Reject paths outside workspace.
Require confirmation before writes.
Disable shell commands by default.
Redact environment variables from logs.
Add tests for malicious inputs.
Fail closed when model output is malformed.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Lab 4 Final Connection
Your submission should include:

Specific failure modes.
Risk ratings.
Concrete mitigations.
At least two safeguards or safety tests.
Production readiness plan.
Copilot-assisted analysis corrections.

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Studio Activity
Threat-model your Java agent:

What input could cause harm?
What tool could be misused?
What output could mislead a user?
What safeguard would fail safely?

title: Module 8: Security and Reliability
text:
Takeaways

Generated code is not security-reviewed code.
Reliability requires explicit design for failure.
Safeguards must be implemented and tested, not merely described.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Learning Outcomes

Evaluate capabilities and limitations of autonomous agents.
Compare agent-based and traditional workflows.
Assess risks of increased autonomy.
Justify appropriate use of agents.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
From Copilot to Agent
In-IDE assistants operate under close human supervision.
Autonomous or background agents take a larger task and work through multiple steps.
This shifts the main question from “Can it write code?” to “Can we safely delegate this workflow?”

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Agent Loop
Common autonomous workflow:

Plan.
Execute.
Verify.
Report.
Receive human feedback.

Your course agent should make this loop inspectable.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Agent Control Points

sequenceDiagram
  participant H as Human
  participant A as Agent
  participant T as Tools
  participant E as Evaluator
  H->>A: Task and constraints
  A->>H: Plan for approval
  H->>A: Approve or revise
  A->>T: Execute allowed tools
  T-->>A: Results
  A->>E: Run checks
  E-->>A: Pass or fail
  A->>H: Report, diff, evidence




sequenceDiagram
  participant H as Human
  participant A as Agent
  participant T as Tools
  participant E as Evaluator
  H->>A: Task and constraints
  A->>H: Plan for approval
  H->>A: Approve or revise
  A->>T: Execute allowed tools
  T-->>A: Results
  A->>E: Run checks
  E-->>A: Pass or fail
  A->>H: Report, diff, evidence

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Appropriate Tasks
Good candidates:

Small, well-scoped changes.
Clear tests or checks.
Low-risk refactors.
Documentation updates.
Repetitive maintenance.

Poor candidates:

Ambiguous architecture decisions.
Security-critical changes without review.
Large rewrites.
Changes with weak verification.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Autonomy Risks

Wrong plan executed confidently.
Tool misuse.
Hidden side effects.
Incomplete verification.
Sunk-cost bias toward bad generated work.
Concurrent agent changes conflicting with humans.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Human Oversight Checkpoints
Meaningful checkpoints happen before irreversible or high-risk action.
Examples:

Approve plan before edits.
Approve file writes.
Review failing tests before retry loops.
Approve PR merge.
Require human signoff for sandbox exceptions.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Testing Strategy for Agents
Agents need tests at multiple levels:

Unit tests for tool dispatcher.
Integration tests for tool-result handling.
Golden examples for workflow output.
Safety tests for denied operations.
Manual review for architecture decisions.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Lab 5 Final Connection
Your controlled workflow should demonstrate:

Core Java agent loop.
Connected tool declarations and dispatcher.
Reproducible multi-step workflow.
Human oversight checkpoint.
Example run artifacts.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Final Milestone 1 Connection
The proposal and architecture due this module should show how Labs 1-5 become a team project.
Your architecture should identify:

Input.
Generation.
Evaluation.
Workflow.
Persistence.
Human oversight.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Studio Activity
Evaluate a candidate task for delegation.
Classify it:

Safe to automate.
Safe with checkpoint.
Human-only decision.

Explain the evidence needed for each classification.

title: Module 10: Autonomous Agents
text:
Takeaways

More autonomy requires stronger controls.
Plan-execute-verify-report must be visible.
Human review remains central to agentic workflows.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Learning Outcomes

Design and implement a complete AI-assisted system.
Integrate workflows, evaluation, and safeguards.
Justify architectural and engineering decisions.
Present and defend the system.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Final System Expectations
Your Controlled Coding Agent should demonstrate:

Structured input handling.
AI-assisted generation.
Evaluation and testing.
Controlled workflow management.
Persistence or artifact tracking.
Documentation.
Human oversight.
Collaborative engineering practice.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Final Submission Timeline

  dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD

  Integration testing        :a2, after a1, 2d
  section Evidence

  AI assistance documentation:b2, after a1, 2d
  section Delivery
  Demo recording             :c1, after a2, 1d
  Final cleanup              :c2, after c1, 1d

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Reproducibility
A grader should be able to answer:

How do I set it up?
How do I run it?
What example should I try?
What output should I expect?
How do I run tests?
Where are evaluation results?
Where is AI assistance documented?

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Evaluation Report
The evaluation report should include:

What was evaluated.
Metrics or criteria.
Test cases or scenarios.
Results.
Interpretation.
Known limitations.
What would improve next.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
AI Assistance Section
Your README should make AI use transparent:

Planning.
Code generation.
Test generation.
Debugging.
Review.
Integration support.
Documentation support.
Accepted, modified, and rejected suggestions.
Verification methods.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Demo Structure
Recommended 5-8 minute flow:

Problem and users.
Architecture overview.
Live or recorded workflow run.
Evaluation results.
Safeguards and human oversight.
Team process and AI evidence.
Lessons learned.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Professional Defense
Be ready to explain:

Why this architecture?
What did AI help with?
What did humans correct?
What risks remain?
Which tests matter most?
What would you do next with more time?

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Final Quality Checklist

Clean repository organization.
No committed secrets.
Fresh-environment setup tested.
Tests and examples run.
Demo link included.
Required document paths submitted.
Commit hash captured.
Individual contributions clear.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Course Synthesis
Responsible AI-assisted engineering means:

Clear intent.
Structured prompts.
Human review.
Automated evidence.
Risk awareness.
Transparent documentation.
Team accountability.

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Closing Reflection
Answer individually:

What AI interaction changed your process most?
What risk did you underestimate early?
What verification habit will you keep?
What work should remain human-led?

title: Module 12: Final Project Workbench
text:
Takeaways

The final project is a demonstration of engineering judgment.
AI assistance is strongest when controlled, evaluated, and documented.
The goal is not to show that AI wrote code; it is to show that your team built trustworthy software with AI assistance.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 5
Points: 100
This lab extends Lab 1: Structured AI Workflow. Continue working in the same comp331-java-agent repository you created for Lab 1; do not create a new repository for this lab.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Build the evaluation layer of your coding agent
Define measurable correctness criteria
Detect regressions in AI-generated outputs
Create the Java sandbox project that later agent tools will inspect and modify

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Extend your Lab 1 comp331-java-agent repository by adding a small Java sandbox project and a repeatable evaluation process. Later labs will give the coding agent tools that operate on this sandbox, so keep it simple and easy to test.

Create a sandbox Java project or package inside your repository. A recommended name is workspace/calculator or sandbox/calculator.
Implement a small Java program or library that has clear behavior. Recommended options include a calculator, string formatter, unit converter, password validator, or CSV summary utility.
Add JUnit tests that check normal cases, edge cases, and at least one invalid-input case.
Define measurable acceptance criteria in your README. Each criterion should connect to a test, command, or review check.
Run the evaluation manually from the command line using mvn test or an equivalent command. Save the output or summarize it in your README.
Use Copilot or another approved AI tool to make at least two iterations of the sandbox code. One iteration may intentionally be incomplete or flawed.
Run the evaluation against at least two versions or iterations of the code and document what changed. You may use separate commits, branches, screenshots, copied terminal output, or a written comparison.
Connect the evaluation back to your Lab 1 structured prompt/specification. Note which acceptance criteria were enforced automatically and which still required human review.

Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn -q test
git log --oneline
Minimum viable version: one sandbox Java class and at least five meaningful JUnit tests. Stretch goal: add a small script or Maven profile that runs only the sandbox evaluation.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository from Lab 1. Continue in that repository instead of creating a new one.
Create the sandbox project, JUnit evaluation, and Copilot-assisted iterations described in the linked instructions.
Document acceptance criteria, test commands, iteration results, Copilot-assisted test or code changes, and verification results in README.md.
Commit your completed work and obtain the Git commit hash for the submitted version.
Submit the repository link, commit hash, and required evaluation evidence through Canvas.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot to strengthen the evaluation process, not to bypass it. You remain responsible for deciding which tests matter and whether the implementation satisfies the assignment.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot Chat to propose JUnit tests before implementation. Review the suggestions and keep only meaningful cases.
Ask Copilot to identify missing edge cases after your first test set is written.
Use Copilot to help create one incomplete or flawed implementation, then use your tests to expose the weakness.
Label tests or test ideas as human-authored, Copilot-assisted, or Copilot-suggested but modified.
Record how each accepted Copilot suggestion was verified with mvn test or an equivalent command.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

A Java sandbox project or package that later labs can inspect and modify.
Evaluation criteria in a documented format.
Runnable JUnit tests or an equivalent automated evaluation command.
Example evaluation results from at least two code iterations.
README instructions for running the evaluations from a clean environment.
Notes explaining what the evaluation caught and what it did not catch.
A short note identifying which test ideas or implementation changes were Copilot-assisted and how they were verified.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
6 Grading
Refer to the assignment rubric in Canvas.

title: Lab 2: Eval-Driven Development
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link and any required evaluation output through Canvas. Identify the exact command your professor should run to execute the evaluation. Submit the Git commit hash for the exact version you want graded. After committing your final work, run:
git rev-parse HEAD
Next lab: Lab 3: AI System Implementation.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
Course Name/Number: COMP 331: AI-Assisted Software Engineering
Week: 9
Points: 90
This lab analyzes the system built in Lab 3: AI System Implementation. Continue working in the same comp331-java-agent repository you used for Labs 1-3; do not create a new repository for this lab.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
1 Purpose of Assignment
This assignment is intended to help you:

Analyze failure modes in your coding agent
Evaluate reliability, security, and maintainability
Propose improvements to increase robustness
Strengthen the safety controls around Java tools before adding an autonomous loop

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
2 Steps / Requirements of Assignment
Conduct a risk analysis of your current Java coding agent workflow in your existing comp331-java-agent repository. Focus on realistic failures in your own implementation rather than generic AI risks. Your system now has tools that can inspect files, read files, write files, and run Java evaluation commands, so your analysis should be specific to those capabilities.

Review each tool from Lab 3 and identify what could go wrong if an LLM requested unsafe or incorrect arguments.
Identify at least six failure modes. Include path traversal, accidental overwrite, command execution risk, secret exposure, misleading model output, incomplete tests, and human oversight gaps where relevant.
Analyze reliability, security, maintainability, and human oversight risks. At least one risk must come from each category.
Rate each risk by likelihood and impact using a simple scale such as Low, Medium, or High.
Propose concrete mitigations. Examples include path normalization, read-size limits, command allowlists, write approval checkpoints, max iteration limits, logging, and better test coverage.
Implement at least two small safeguards or tests based on your analysis. Reasonable options include blocking absolute paths, adding a command allowlist, adding a write confirmation mode, or adding tests for secret-like files.
Add a production-readiness checklist that distinguishes what is currently safe enough for coursework from what would be required before real use.
Identify which mitigations you plan to carry into Lab 5 and the final project.

Suggested risk table columns:












Risk
Tool/Component
Category
Likelihood
Impact
Current Control
Proposed Mitigation





Suggested checkpoint commands:
mvn test
mvn test -Dtest=*Tool*

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
3 Action Items

Click the link to the full hosted lab instructions: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness.
Follow the instructions in the lab page.
Open your existing comp331-java-agent repository and review each Lab 3 tool implementation. Continue in that repository instead of creating a new one.
Complete the risk analysis, safeguard implementation, and safety-test work described in the linked instructions.
Document the risk table, production-readiness checklist, prioritized improvement plan, Copilot-assisted analysis notes, and verification results in the repository.
Commit your completed work and obtain the Git commit hash for the submitted version.
Submit the repository link, commit hash, and risk-analysis document through Canvas.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
4 Using GitHub Copilot
Use GitHub Copilot as a risk-analysis assistant, not as the final authority. Your risk analysis must be specific to your own Java tool implementation.
Recommended Copilot workflow:

Ask Copilot to identify failure modes for your actual file, path, write, and test-execution tools.
Verify Copilot’s suggestions against your code and tests. Remove generic risks that do not apply.
Ask Copilot to critique your proposed mitigations, then decide which safeguards are realistic for this project.
Include one example where Copilot missed, understated, or overstated a risk.

Suggested prompt:
Review this Java coding-agent tool design for realistic risks.
Focus on reliability, security, maintainability, and human oversight.
Identify concrete failure modes tied to file reading, file writing, path handling, and test execution.
Do not give generic AI risk language.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
5 Deliverables
Your repository should include:

Risk analysis document or README section specific to your Java coding agent.
Failure mode table with likelihood, impact, current controls, and mitigation.
Evidence of at least two implemented safeguards or safety tests.
Production-readiness checklist.
Prioritized improvement plan for Lab 5 and the final project.
Copilot risk-analysis notes, including one example where you corrected or refined Copilot’s analysis.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
6 Grading
Refer to the assignment rubric in Canvas.

title: Lab 4: AI Risk Analysis and Production Readiness
text:
7 Submission Instructions
Submit your repository link and risk analysis document through Canvas. Identify any safeguards you implemented in code or tests. Submit the Git commit hash for the exact version you want graded. After committing your final work, run:
git rev-parse HEAD
